name: vscode-worktree-hot-swap
description: Switch between git worktrees in a single VS Code window without reloading, carrying your open editor tabs (with cursor/scroll position) over to the equivalent files in the target worktree. Designed for use with Claude Code.

colors.description: Foreground color of the current worktree in the Worktrees view.

commands.title: Worktree Hot Swap: Open Terminal in Worktree
Worktree Hot Swap: Debug Dump (log state)

worktree-hot-swap.carryTabs.description: When switching worktrees, close tabs open against files in the old worktree and reopen the equivalent files in the new worktree, preserving cursor/scroll position and focus.

worktree-hot-swap.persistPositionsAcross.description: Persist cached cursor/scroll positions in globalState so they survive VS Code restarts (not just in-session).

worktree-hot-swap.anchorOnOpen.description: When a single git worktree is opened as the workspace, establish the anchor layout immediately on open (a one-time extension-host restart). This moves the unavoidable setup restart into the initial load, so the first worktree switch is instantaneous. Disable to defer setup until the first switch instead.

worktree-hot-swap.languageServerRestartC.markdownDescription:
Command IDs to run (debounced) to re-scope language servers to the active worktree after a switch or after reconciling stray tabs. Many language servers keep the previous worktree's index across a folder swap, so without this, Go to Definition can resolve a shared symbol (e.g. a function declared in a header) to the wrong worktree's copy.

The default covers common servers for each major language, including alternates where a language has more than one popular server (e.g. clangd *and* cpptools, Pylance/Jedi *and* basedpyright, tsserver *and* Deno, Roslyn *and* OmniSharp, Vue *and* Vetur); **commands that aren't registered are skipped**, so only servers whose extension is actually running are restarted — unused entries cost nothing and never activate a dormant extension, and `#worktree-hot-swap.languageServerRestartGuards#` additionally skips servers that are installed but configured off. Add your own language server's restart command here if it isn't listed. Set to `[]` to disable. There is no VS Code API to enumerate language servers, so this list is necessarily curated.

setting.description: Full setting ID to inspect, e.g. clangd.enable.

skipValues.description: Skip the restart when the setting's current value equals any of these.

worktree-hot-swap.languageServerRestartG.markdownDescription:
Map of `restart command` → guard. Before running a command from `#worktree-hot-swap.languageServerRestartCommands#`, if it has a guard here and the guarded setting's current value is one of `skipValues`, the restart is skipped.

This exists because a *registered* restart command is not proof the server is in use: some extensions keep the command registered while their server is configured off and answer it with UI or side effects — vscode-clangd with `clangd.enable: false` pops an "enable clangd?" prompt, cpptools with `C_Cpp.intelliSenseEngine: "disabled"` shows a warning toast, and Zig's `zig.zls.startRestart` force-writes `zig.zls.enabled: "on"` into user settings. The default guards cover all three, so a server you keep installed-but-disabled is never poked. (Edge case: if you run ZLS via an explicit `zig.zls.path` while `zig.zls.enabled` is not `"on"`, relax the Zig guard.)

worktree-hot-swap.languageServerReadines.markdownDescription:
Map of `restart command` → `extension id`. After running a restart command (from `#worktree-hot-swap.languageServerRestartCommands#`), the extension watches the named extension's exported language client to know when the server is Running again — so a rapid sequence of switches can fire an immediate coalesced follow-up restart instead of waiting a fixed ~6s gap.

The client is read from the extension's `exports` via `getApi(1).languageClient`, `languageClient`, or `client` (in that order). Commands **without** an entry here, extensions whose client can't be found, and entries for extensions that **aren't installed** simply fall back to the fixed time gap — nothing breaks.

*clangd note:* vscode-clangd 0.6.0's `clangd.restart` handler awaits the new client's creation and repoints the exported `languageClient` before the command resolves, so the readiness observation sees the fresh client; the probe is transition-aware to handle other servers/versions that resolve their restart while the old client is still winding down.

scripts.compile: npm run check-types && npm run lint && node esbuild.js
scripts.package: npm run check-types && npm run lint && node esbuild.js --production
scripts.pretest:integration: npm run compile-tests && npm run compile && npm run lint
